comment_content:
I wanna get my story out. I remember being at the concert as soon as stargazing started to play I remember about eight people in my section started to fall so I already picked up 4 people from the ground  when the first song started to play, 
  My brother was right next to me. He picked up 3 people from the ground and I remember in the song stargazing the second verse a huge beat drops and this kid fell down and I got this feeling like if I don’t pick him up, he could get stomped on pretty badly, as soon as I pick him up off the ground, the beat drops and I’m carrying his body with me and we’re bouncing up and down. My brother still next to me during this part ,we try to hold hands to stay close. We got a hard grip we look at each other ,and jokingly we’re telling each other,” I just saved another life!” 
  I picked 5 people up from the ground that night from the first song , my brother picked up 3 , 

crazy because saving lives is what we actually did and in the moment we didn’t realize the extent of what just happened. 
 
It was so tight, Very hard to move and the crowd eventually separated my brother and I , there was no shot we could stick together, I remember, trying to navigate a way out of the crowd and I couldn’t believe how never ending the crowd was , it was bigger than I knew . 

We were all safe and were shocked when the concert was over as soon as we got to the hotel it was already reported that people died .
 
It was a unique time to be at a concert. Covid had just happened. We hadn’t been at a show in over a year, going on nearly 2 years. There were a lot of foreshadowing going on during the prior concerts like Don Toliver , women couldn’t breathe and things like that , but because of the uniqueness of not being at a show in so long, we were having trouble acknowledging like people not breathing well, was that OK? Was it not? We were all unsure because nobody was expecting death , we all came to Astro world to have the greatest time , ever! 
Nobody was hurt at those Don Toliver concerts , but it, was raising some red flags and questions, and foreshadowing to what was to come that night.
reply_content: The problem was how UNHINGED people were. Not Travis. Just immature, impatient, unruly kids. SMH

comment_content: So you wanna blame someone? Next time you see LIVE NATION blasted across on every concert ticket you buy, remember they're at fault for killing those kids & that they still are among the largest concert producers in the world and that Michael Rapino, who was the CEO when this went down, is STILL the CEO. Live Nation should be out of business for their inability to make key decisions that night.... kids were literally buried alive by bodies because of their blatant disregard for safety, complete neglect and utterly unprofessional handling for their concert-goers. Ya'll need to NEVER give Live Nation another dollar and pressure your favorite artists into boycotting that monopoly- oh, and just so you know: they're the reason you can't afford to see shows in the US anymore (they're part-owned by ticketmaster). NO artist should have ever signed on to work with them again after this, but they're so powerful they made this all just go away- it took 4 years to make this doc- was an EGREGIOUS and PREVENTABLE tragedy and they should've been outright shut down from JUST the lawsuits from this incident alone, but instead they're one of the wealthiest companies in the US, worth $33.25 billion. Fck Live Nation.
reply_content: To clear things up: it's not "the venue" that decides how a crowd moves at an event, it's the concert production company, Live Nation. There's a whole production team they hire, whose literal job is to design not just for aesthetics, but for ingress/egress and the safety for thousands of innocent people at EVERY big festival and concert they produce. Travis Scott hired THEM to handle the logistics- it's not the job of the artist AT ALL- in fact it's the producer's job to shut down any creative ideas that could impede safety of the audience (pyrotechnics, crowd flow, evacuation plans, emergency weather plans, EMS access, etc.), including gatecrashing. Watch the doc to see the MANY ways Live Nation utterly failed to do their job. Throwing a festival is a huge responsibility- so much gets decided behind the scenes and whether it was Live Nation being cheap, passing blame, hiring amateurs or pure arrogance, they dropped the ball hard and those 10 kids died horrible, terrifying and senseless deaths & many more were traumatized on their watch.

reply_content: What happened at Astroworld would be nearly impossible in Poland. Our laws around mass events are extremely strict. Organizers must get official approval from the city, provide certified security personnel, limit the number of attendees based on venue size, and ensure clear, marked evacuation routes. Medical teams and ambulances must be present on-site. Every event requires a detailed safety and evacuation plan reviewed by police, fire services, and emergency medical authorities. An event this chaotic would never be allowed to happen here — it would be shut down before it even started.
